revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:人制]][[ko:인제 (일본)]]
'''Hito-sei''' (人制) refers to a proto-administrative structure believed to have existed in the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato polity|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2658252}} during the {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|qid=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}}.
== Overview == During the 5th century, the Yamato polity had a system where {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} oversaw {{ill|Be (social group)|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be (social group)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=Bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2892644}}, organizing national governance. By this time, select individuals among the tomonomiyatsuko who excelled in their duties were detached from their family or regional communities to serve at designated locations. These individuals formed groups called "X-person" (×人), establishing ties with the ruling authority and assuming specific official roles.<ref>Ken Shinokawa, Ryuichi Okawara, Masanobu Suzuki, eds., "Research on the Kuni-no-miyatsuko and Be Systems" (Yagi Shoten, 2017).</ref>
Inscriptions on artifacts such as the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11596864}}'s {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|qid=Q856395|lt=Inariyama sword|1=Inariyama Sword}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|qid=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}} bear the title "Jōtōjin" (杖刀人), and the inscription on a {{ill|大刀|en|大刀|qid=Q1224104|lt=Tachi|1=大刀}} from the {{ill|Eta Funayama Kofun|en|Eta Funayama Kofun|qid=Q11259227|lt=Eda Funayama Kofun|1=Eta Funayama Kofun}} in {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} mentions "Tensōjin" (典曹人). Similar titles also appeared in the 6th-century {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} kingdom, with some scholars proposing an influence from China's {{ill|Rites of Zhou|en|Rites of Zhou|qid=Q1367984|lt=Zhouli|1=Rites of Zhou}} administrative system.
The historian {{ill|Naoki Kōjirō|ja|直木孝次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki Kōjirō|1=Kōjirō Naoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581787}} coined the term "Hito-sei" to refer to this official system, and it has since been widely used in historical studies.
Records up to the {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|qid=Q8086|lt=8th century|1=8th century}}, including those in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, mention numerous titles and clan names that include "X-person" (×人), suggesting that such positions were inherited. By the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|qid=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}, these designations were replaced with '''Tomo''' (とも/とものお), which later evolved into the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}'s {{ill|Befu (government service)|ja|伴部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Befu (government service)|1=tomobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17222957}}.
When comparing the documented Hito-sei system with the later Ritsuryō-era bureaucratic divisions, some positions appear to have evolved into official government offices: * '''Sakajin''' (酒人) → {{ill|Sake Brewers Office|ja|造酒司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sake Brewers Office|1=Brewing Department|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11639771}} Sake Department * '''Kurajin''' (倉人) → {{ill|Ōkurashō|ja|大蔵省|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkurashō|1=Ministry of the Treasury|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q910073}} and {{ill|Nakatsukasa-shō|ja|内蔵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsukasa-shō|1=kuraryou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11394423}} Storage Department * '''Tejin''' (手人) → Bureau of Craftsmen within the Ōkurashō and Nakatsukasa-shō * '''Shishijin''' (宍人) → {{ill|Imperial Table Office|ja|内膳司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Table Office|1=Naizen-shi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11394413}} Catering Department * '''Hiyajin''' (氷人) → {{ill|Waterworks Office|ja|主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waterworks Office|1=Main Water Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11368771}} Ice Management Department ==See Also== * [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no miyatsuko]] * [[Japanese administrative system]] * {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji-Kabane system|qq=}} * {{ill|Yatsuka no Kabane|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsuka no Kabane|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Naoki Kōjirō, "Hito-sei," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6426904}} 11 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1990) ISBN 978-4-642-00511-1 * Yagi Mitsuru, "Hito-sei," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524488|1=Nihon Rekishi Daijiten}} 3 (Shogakukan, 2001) ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0 * Naoki Kōjirō, "Research on Hito-sei," in *Structure of the Ancient Japanese State*, Aoki Shoten, 1985 * Shinokawa Ken, "Be System," in *Research on the Ancient Japanese Kuni-no-miyatsuko System*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1996 * Yoshimura Takehiko, "Wa Kingdom and the Yamato Court," in *Iwanami Lecture on Japanese History* 2, Iwanami Shoten, 1993 * Suzuki Yasuhiko, "The Diplomacy and Internal Affairs of the Five Kings of Wa," in *Development of the Wa State and East Asia*, Iwanami Shoten, 2012 * Tanaka Fumio, "The Five Kings of Wa and Their Rule over the Archipelago," in *Iwanami Lecture on Japanese History* 1, Iwanami Shoten, 2013 * Hiraishi Mitsuru, "Reconsidering Hito-sei," in *Shimane Prefectural Ancient Culture Center Research Journal* 14, 2015 * Mizoguchi Yuki, "Hito-sei, Be System, and Regional Society," in *Ancient Japanese Regions and Social Integration*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2015 * Horikawa Toru, "From Hito-sei to Be System," in *Research on the Kuni-no-miyatsuko and Be Systems*, Yagi Shoten, 2017 * Suzuki Masanobu, "Current Status and Challenges of Hito-sei Research," in *Ancient Japanese Kuni-no-miyatsuko and Regional Society*, Yagi Shoten, 2023
{{translated page|ja|人制|version=104426504|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:人制]]
